coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and dendrimers nanohybrids: from synthesis to applications
coredata.dc:creator: Martins, Ivo J.
Tomás, Helena
Rodrigues, João
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots and dendrimers have captured significant attention in the scientific community due to their remarkable properties and potential applications in various fields. These nanomaterials have shown unique characteristics that make them fascinating subjects of study and research. Their combination to form nanohybrid materials takes advantage of their individual attributes and can originate powerful nanoplatforms—either in the form of particles or films—with improved and unexpected properties. The state-of-art regarding these new nanohybrid platforms, including the synthesis strategies and characterization methods applied to evaluate their properties and nanohybrid nature, is presented. The potential applications of these nanohybrid materials in biomedicine, sensors, and solar cells are also highlighted. Lastly, a vision for the future of this field is presented, focusing on the design towards optimization of their function.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoplatforms
Nanocarriers
Biomedicine
Sensors
Solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots and dendrimers have captured significant attention in the scientific community due to their remarkable properties and potential applications in various fields. These nanomaterials have shown unique characteristics that make them fascinating subjects of study and research. Their combination to form nanohybrid materials takes advantage of their individual attributes and can originate powerful nanoplatforms—either in the form of particles or films—with improved and unexpected properties. The state-of-art regarding these new nanohybrid platforms, including the synthesis strategies and characterization methods applied to evaluate their properties and nanohybrid nature, is presented. The potential applications of these nanohybrid materials in biomedicine, sensors, and solar cells are also highlighted. Lastly, a vision for the future of this field is presented, focusing on the design towards optimization of their function.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells
Generation zero, one, two, or three

ce:section.ce:para: Hybrid nanomaterials have garnered significant attention from the scientific community over the years due to their unique ability to leverage the distinct properties of their constituents. By exploiting this feature, hybrid nanomaterials can exhibit novel behaviors and improve performance, expanding their potential applications [1–4]. Nanomaterials like carbon dots (CDs) and dendrimers are among the most explored due to their captivating properties and potential use. CDs were discovered in 2004 by Xu and Scrivens [5] when purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes by gel-electrophoresis. The fluorescent detected impurities (small fragments) were called “fluorescent carbon”. Two years later, Sun et al. [6] named these new carbon nanomaterials “carbon dots”, concluding that probably due to the quantum-size effects, particles with 30–50 nm were much less fluorescent than smaller ones. CDs are a general designation that describes distinct carbon nanosized particles that exhibit unique physicochemical and photophysical properties. Based on the different attributes, a new classification has been introduced to describe the CDs, and four distinct nanostructures arose: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs) [7]. Graphene quantum dots are structured similarly to a honeycomb network with sp2 hybridized carbon. In contrast, CQDs consist of various layers of sp2/sp3 carbon sheets that are π–π stacked—both with graphitic carbon arrangements and functional groups placed on their surface. Moreover, GQDs are shaped similarly to a disc (<20 nm), and CQDs are of spherical/quasi-spherical shape (1–10 nm). CNDs have an amorphous carbon core with surface functional groups, and dimensions are <10 nm. Concerning the PDs, the internal structure and surface are typically polymeric, and even patterns of amorphous to semi-graphitic cores can be found in these carbon nanomaterials. The design and preparation of all these CDs can be regulated by the synthesis conditions, namely by selecting the synthesis method, which can correspond to a top-down (usually GQDs) or bottom-up approach (for CQDs, CNDs, or PDs). The type of starting precursor can also influence the synthesis (e.g., aromatic or polymeric type of molecules), as well as the temperature and the reaction time, which allow the control of the carbonization degree. Photoluminescence (PL) is a crucial characteristic shared by all forms of carbon mentioned. While the origins and mechanisms of PL are still an open question, researchers have identified the surface as a critical factor influencing the PL properties of these materials [8]. Other assets attributed to CDs are related to their water-solubility, surface functionality, and low cytotoxicity. Further details on the nomenclature, structure elucidation, properties, design, and methods of synthesis of CDs can be found in several recent reviews [7,9,10]. Interested readers can refer to these publications for in-depth insights into the various aspects of CDs. Considering their relatively late discovery, CDs already have found a significant number of potential applications, such as in biomedicine (drug and gene delivery), sensorial detection (metal ions, biomolecules, drugs, or explosive compounds), and energy conversion and storage, through solar cells and rechargeable batteries [11–18].

As far as dendrimers are concerned, they were first synthesized by Fritz Vögtle [19] in 1978 through a divergent method and later baptized as “dendrimers” by Donald Tomalia [20]. By definition, dendrimers are monodispersed polymeric branched nanoarchitectures that are mainly classified using the nomenclature and terminology developed by George Newkome [21]. These macromolecules are composed of a central core unit on which the dendritic structure is built; generations, regular layers like onion skins; dendrons, branched structures connected like a section to the central unit; and terminal groups [22]. The most common types of studied dendrimers are the poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM), poly(propyleneimine) (PPI) [23], poly(phosphorus) [24], poly(triazines) [25] and silicon-based dendrimers [26], among others. The dendrimer chemical nature and structure define their shape, stability, conformational flexibility, and viscosity. A peculiar feature of dendrimers is the decrease in viscosity when the number of generations increases due to the tendency to acquire a globular and compact form, contrasting to the open-arm structure in lower generations. These dragon-tree or dandelion-like structures presenting low polydispersity have controlled-molecular weight, water solubility, surface functionality, and empty internal cavities (which can be used to transport cargo), making them powerful candidates for biomedical and veterinary applications [27–33]. In addition to these applications, dendrimers can act as capping agents of different nanomaterials, particularly metallic and magnetic nanoparticles [34], as well as semiconductor quantum dots [35], contributing to the stabilization of their physicochemical properties, and even act as nanoreactors for the synthesis of, for instance, gold or copper nanoparticles [36]. Dendrimers have established themselves as a promising class of materials in academic research, having demonstrated significant achievements over the years. Their unique properties and versatile applications have made them a popular subject of study, paving the way for numerous breakthroughs. However, major issues, such as large-scale synthesis, purification, and cytotoxicity, among others, have limited their application and, for instance, delayed their arrival at the clinic [37,38]. As for CDs, and although they are relatively young when compared to other carbon-based nanomaterials, they already possess certain limitations, like achieving a satisfying level of control over the synthesis (in terms of size, surface functional groups, and PL of the CDs), the absence of a clear knowledge regarding the fluorescence mechanism, and the low synthesis yields [39,40]. Yet, despite the apparent weaknesses of these two nanomaterials, the combination of their individual attributes (like fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, loading-capabilities, and surface functionality) can be used to originate efficient nanohybrid systems, with their terminal functionalities being vital for the nanohybrid formation.

Most current research on nanohybrids with CDs involves metals and their oxides, carbon nitrides, and hydrogels, as evidenced by several studies [41–47]. However, apart from a 2021 review paper that primarily examines the construction of CDs/PAMAM dendrimer nanohybrids for biomedical applications [48], there appears to be a lack of comprehensive reviews investigating the synthesis of nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers and their potential applications in different fields. This review goes beyond the biomedical field by presenting and analyzing research published over the last decade on the synthesis and applications of nanohybrid materials formed between CDs and dendrimers. A preliminary analysis of the number of published papers in the last 10 years (Fig. 1 ) indicates that this is a relatively unexplored field with significant potential for growth and expansion. Therefore, this review will start by emphasizing synthesis strategies and delving into the various methods used to prepare and characterize nanohybrids (in the form of nanoparticles or nanofilms) using different dendrimer types. Subsequently, this review will highlight the potential applications of these nanohybrids in the fields of biomedicine, sensors, and solar cells. Finally, the key findings of this study will be summarized, and an outlook on the future of this specific area will be presented.

The most common way to prepare nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers using the covalent method was by forming bonds through amide coupling chemistry or disulfide bonding (Table 1 ). The covalent method permits the development of nanohybrids with stronger affinities, consequently with better physicochemical stability, potential PL enhancements, and more uniform size-dispersed nanohybrids.

Amide coupling chemistry relies on carbodiimide agents such as EDC/NHS (1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide/N-hydroxysuccinimide) or DCC (N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide) to activate the surface of CDs and covalently attach the dendrimer, forming a covalent-type nanohybrid. To our knowledge, the first research paper reporting the covalent bonding between CDs and dendrimers was published in 2013 and reported the preparation of Au-PAMAM-CDs nanohybrids by amide coupling. Au colloids were prior functionalized with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and then linked to the PAMAM dendrimers by amide bonding. This reaction step was followed by the addition of CDs that reacted for 24 h at room temperature to generate the desired Au-PAMAM-CDs nanohybrids [49]. In another study, carbon nanodots previously prepared from dehydrating starch nanoparticles with sulfuric acid were activated with EDC/NHS to react with both G0 and G1 PAMAM-NH2 dendrimers (Fig. 4 ). The covalent bonding was either achieved at room temperature for 6 h or using the microwave at 60 W for 15 min at 75 °C [50]. In a different work, commercial graphene quantum dots (GQDs) having surface carboxylic groups were conjugated with G3 PAMAM-NH2 dendrimers by carbodiimide coupling chemistry (EDC/NHS), generating a nanohybrid of three main components: graphene quantum dots, PAMAM dendrimers, and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the detection of cardiac biomarkers [51].

Feng and collaborators [52] also used the same PAMAM dendrimer generation to prepare a nanohybrid with red-fluorescent CDs. First, the procedure involved mixing citric acid and formamide to synthesize the CDs in a stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor. Then, the CDs were dispersed in methanol and covalently bonded to G3 PAMAM-NH2 using EDC/NHS. In a different study, peptide dendritic CDs for anticancer applications were developed. Primarily, the CDs were synthesized using glucose as a single starting precursor and then microwaved at 800 W for 3 min. The surface was modified with sulfhydryl groups (CDs-SH) to receive G3 peptide dendrimers by disulfide exchange reaction forming CD-S-S-peptide dendrimer covalent nanohybrids [53]. Using low-generation PAMAM dendrimers (G1, G2, and G3) and carboxyl-CDs, other covalent-type nanohybrids were formed. Here, the CDs were first prepared from sweet lemon peel through hydrothermal carbonization. The conjugation between CDs and G1-G3 PAMAM dendrimers was assisted by EDC/NHS to form three individual nanohybrids, named CDP1, CDP2, and CDP3 [54]. Using a different approach, Pooresmaeil and co-workers [55], used GQDs that were previously functionalized with (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane-APTES) forming GQDs-NH2, to grow PAMAM-NH2 dendrimer on the modified surface. The dendrimer (G3) growth was achieved by Michael and amidation reactions using methyl acrylate and ethylenediamine, respectively. As for Karimi et al. [56], they coupled graphene quantum dots with G3 triazine dendrimers. The triazine dendritic structure was grown on the surface of magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles and further conjugated with GQDs using DCC. After preparation, the final nanoplatform was named core-shell structural Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. Also, the same research group recently functionalized magnetic Fe3O4 NPs with triazine dendrimer and folic acid-derived CDs [57]. First, the CDs were synthesized using folic acid as starting material and the hydrothermal method, originating N-doped CDs. These were further covalently bonded to the carboxyl group of triazine magnetic dendrimers by DCC coupling chemistry for 24 h at room temperature, resulting in a magnetic nanoplatform, named Fe3O4@SiO2-G2.5–CDs, for targeted delivery of doxorubicin (DOX). The described studies show that covalent nanohybrids were mostly formed by amide or disulfide bonding reactions using low generation dendrimers (G0-G4) for that purpose. These generations possess less steric effects, and their open-arm structure increases the interaction points with the surfaces and can even enhance the PL of CDs. The drawbacks of the covalent method are related to the experimental synthesis, requiring more time to optimize the reaction parameters and further purification. Another important aspect to consider in the synthesis of covalent nanohybrids is the specificity of the terminal functional groups in the CDs and dendrimers, as these must be designed unequivocally to establish bonds in mild reaction conditions, therefore avoiding damage to the integrity and properties of both nanomaterials.

Combining CDs and PAMAM dendrimers through a sandwich-type method and taking advantage of antibody-antigen (Ab-Ag) interactions was utilized for immunosensing applications. In this case, an immunosensing platform was developed by combining the two nanomaterials. First, the CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine and surface functionalized with ssCA125DNA (CA125-ovarian cancer antigen) aptamer by EDC/NHS chemistry. The CDs-aptamer recognized Au-PAMAM-antibody-antigen-CA125 forming a sandwich-type nanohybrid [66]. Another immunosensor was also developed using the sandwich method, by coupling CDs and Au-PAMAM dendrimers. Primarily, the CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of histidine and surface functionalized with CA 15-3 (breast cancer marker) antibody by amide coupling chemistry, with further recognition of the CA 15-3 antigen (Fig. 7 ). Then, it formed a complex with AuNPs-PAMAM-aptamer generating a sandwich-type nanohybrid FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer) immunosensor [67].

The nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers have been characterized by different techniques to confirm their formation, assess the interactions between constituents, and evaluate their final properties. The section is divided into optical properties, surface interaction, morphology, and ζ-potential.

The optical properties of the developed nanohybrids are typically monitored by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectroscopy techniques to confirm the successful formation of nanohybrids. Indeed, CDs exhibit two distinctive absorption bands in the UV–Vis spectral range that are attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, along with several surface states. The position of these states depends on the CDs’ synthesis.

The interaction with dendrimers causes new and shifted absorption bands to appear, attesting the nanohybrid formation. For example, GQDs were chemically bonded to G3-triazine dendrimers already linked to magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, originating Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. For the Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer, the presence of absorption bands at 231 and 383 nm is ascribed to the n–π* transitions of C=N bonds and n–π* transitions to the C=O bonds from the dendrimer. While the GQDs present three bands at 230, 276, and 360 nm, attributed to π–π* and n–π* transitions. After the interaction, two UV-bands appeared at 230 and 360 nm attributed to GQDs and Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, suggesting their successful conjugation [56].

Another study showed that upon interaction with the dendrimer, the maximum absorption wavelength of CQDs can shift from 255 to 262 nm. First, single CQDs displayed a unique absorption band at 255 nm, assigned to π–π* transition of the conjugated C=C band, which shifted to 262 nm upon interaction with the G5 PAMAM-acetylated dendrimer, suggesting the interaction of the dendrimer terminal amines with the hydroxyl groups of CQDs. Fluorescence enhancements also occurred when the negatively-charged CQDs were grafted on the PAMAM, increasing the average fluorescence lifetime from 5.8 ns for CQDs to 6.1 ns for CQDs@EPI⊂G5-Ac85 nanohybrids [59]. A study on the interaction between CQDs and G4 PAMAM dendrimers also focused on UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy to characterize the non-covalent nanohybrid. Pristine CQDs presented two absorption bands at 230 and 262 nm, ascribed to π–π*, n–σ*, and n–π* electronic transitions of C=C and C=O/O–H groups, which were shifted to 229 and 272 nm after the formation of CQDs@PAMAM nanohybrids. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs also increased after interaction with G4 PAMAM dendrimers (by 2-fold), as well as the quantum yield (QY), from 3.9 to 6.9%. These enhancements were attributed to the protective effect of the dendrimer on the surface of CQDs and the presence of nitrogen atoms [60]. In other studies, the meticulous analysis of characteristic UV-absorption bands from the CDs, PAMAM dendrimer, encapsulated drug, and target ligands suggests the successful complexation of all the components leading to the formation of nanohybrid platforms [61,62]. Polyaminated CNDs-G0 and CND-G1 nanohybrids prepared by the covalent method were analyzed by PL spectroscopy. Generally, the nanohybrids were good fluorescence emitters, and the CNDs-G0 PAMAM nanohybrids had broader emission than the CNDs-G1 PAMAM, which might be attributed to the polyamination effect [50]. In contrast, fluorescence intensity reductions were reported by Feng et al. [52] for the nanohybrids CDs/G3 PAMAM, accompanied by red-shift emission. The authors attributed this behaviour to amide bond formation. In a study by Li et al. [61], (CDs/DOX)@G5–RGD–TPGS nanohybrids were synthesized using both the covalent and non-covalent methods. The results showed a drastic decrease in the fluorescence for covalent nanohybrids, while slight reductions in the PL properties of non-covalent nanohybrids were verified. Although the QY lowered from 36.8 (pure CDs) to 16.4% for the final complex, the fluorescence was mostly maintained.

Another case of nanohybrids with generation-dependent fluorescence was reported for non-covalent nanohybrids based on CDs and G4-G6 PAMAM dendrimers. The QY of pure CDs (20%) decreased upon interaction with PAMAM dendrimers. QY reductions were also observed as the PAMAM dendrimer generation was increased. QY was 11.9, 10.5, and 7.7% for the G4, G5, and G6 CDs@PAMAM nanohybrids, respectively [63].

From the described studies, the characterization of the optical properties and its impact on the PL of CDs was an important indicator of nanohybrid formation. Generally, PL improvements were observed in both covalent and non-covalent nanohybrids. For covalent nanohybrids, lower dendrimer generations result in PL enhancements. Non-covalent nanohybrids, however, are mainly composed of higher dendrimer generations, and although PL improvements can occur, the main trend is the preservation of the PL properties. A comparative study performed by Li and co-workers [61] (covalent vs. non-covalent synthesis) confirmed that higher dendrimer generations should be preferentially combined with CDs through a non-covalent approach to avoid significant downgrades in the fluorescence of CDs. In fact, the boosting of PL in nanohybrids formed covalently should be further studied in the future, and the QYs evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface interaction assessed by IR and NMR spectroscopies
ce:section.ce:para: The surface interaction between CDs and dendrimers to generate covalent or non-covalent nanohybrids has been characterized by infrared (IR) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopies. Covalent-type nanohybrids were mostly prepared by carbodiimide coupling chemistry, with amide bonds being formed between the CDs and dendrimers. The IR was used to characterize three covalent nanohybrids composed of CDs and G1-G3 PAMAM dendrimers. The vibration bands obtained for the CDs, namely at 3400 and 2934 cm−1, were ascribed to the stretching of O–H and C–H, respectively, and at 1715 cm−1 to the stretching of C=O, signifying the presence of surface carboxylic groups. After conjugating the dendrimer by amide coupling reaction, the band indicative of the carboxylic group disappeared, and new bands arose at 1645 and 3289 cm−1 from amide/amine groups [54]. The surface interaction between GQDs and triazine dendrimers was also monitored by IR. The most important vibration bands for the GQDs were detected at 1725 cm−1 and ascribed to C=O from carboxylic groups. After conjugation with the triazine dendrimer, the presence of new vibration bands at 1557, 1638, and 1701 cm−1 were assigned to NH, CONH, and C=O, suggesting that the dendrimer was grafted to GQDs, forming Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs [56]. Using a different approach, CQDs were coated with G4 PAMAM through the hydrothermal method and analyzed by IR spectroscopy. For the CQDs, the stretching band at 1709 cm−1 was ascribed to carbonyl groups that further disappeared after the dendrimer capping. Other bands from the CQDs also shifted to higher frequencies [68]. Regarding the preparation of non-covalent nanohybrids, the NMR technique was used to confirm the surface interaction between the two components, namely through the presence of representative peaks from both species. For example, in the case of nanohybrids prepared with CQDs and G5 PAMAM-acetylated dendrimers [59]. In summary, since the preparation of covalent-type nanohybrids has been mainly achieved through amide bonds, IR spectroscopy is a useful tool to monitor the new interactions, as new or shifted vibrational bands can be attributed to carbonyl groups from amides and secondary amides. Regarding the non-covalent nanohybrids, NMR spectroscopy seems to be very useful in detecting the presence of representative peaks of both components by comparing pristine vs. shifted peaks displayed by the nanohybrids, allowing for effective identification and characterization of the materials.

The morphology (which includes size and shape) of the developed nanohybrids was mainly characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 8 ), dynamic light scattering (DLS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and NMR diffusometry [69]. The TEM analysis for the covalent nanohybrids (Fig. 8, top) of CND-G0 PAMAM and CND-G1 PAMAM revealed that the nanohybrids presented a round shape, and the morphology was slightly higher for the CND-G1 PAMAM, with diameters between 30 and 60 nm [50]. The nanohybrids linked by the amide coupling reaction of GQDs and G3 PAMAM dendrimer were analyzed by AFM (tapping mode) to examine the topological features and roughness factor (Ra) during the subsequent changes of the electrode. After decoration with GQDs, a densely packed surface morphology was obtained, with a Ra of 6.5 nm. The Ra increased to 8 nm after assembling layers of PAMAM dendrimer, forming well-aligned nanohybrid Au/ATP/GQD/PAMAM layers [51]. The covalent nanohybrids developed by Feng et al. [52] (CDs@PAMAM-G3) were characterized by TEM and DLS, and the results obtained from TEM showed a spherical shape for the nanohybrids and an average size of 27 nm, which is significantly higher than pristine CDs (4–6 nm). In a different approach, the XRD technique was used to predict the size of nanohybrids by using the Debye-Scherrer equation, and the calculated size after grafting GQDs to Fe3O4@C@TD went from 111.5 to 112.4 nm [56]. Non-covalent nanohybrids between CQDs and G4 PAMAM were linked by electrostatic interaction, and the TEM images showed a spherical shape and an average size of 65 nm, higher than naked CQDs (12 nm) [60]. NMR diffusometry was also used to confirm the successful interaction between CDs/DOX and G5–RGD–TPGS by comparing their diffusion rates since the complexes formed diffused slower when compared to single dendrimers and CDs. Moreover, the TEM micrographs (Fig. 8, middle) also showed that CDs/DOX@G5–RGD–TPGS complexes formed clustered conjugates with sizes of 131.8 nm [61]. Also, non-covalent G4-G6 CDs@PAMAM nanohybrids were analyzed by TEM and DLS. TEM analysis (Fig. 8, bottom) showed amorphous agglomerates, where the dark and grey regions corresponded to CDs and PAMAM dendrimers, respectively. By DLS, they verified that the hydrodynamic diameter was 9 nm in the case of CDs and between 20 and 40 nm for the nanohybrids [63].

The ζ-potential is an important indicator of interaction between CDs and dendrimers since the surrounding environment changes when grafting dendrimers on CDs or vice-versa. DLS (or ELS) was used to obtain the ζ-potential values for the interaction between CDs and dendrimers. Usually, anionic-type CDs were preferentially synthesized due to the negative ζ-potentials obtained and combined with cationic-type dendrimers with positive ζ-potentials values. For instance, CDs with a ζ-potential of −3.5 mV were obtained due to the presence of surface carboxylic groups and were combined with G1, G2, and G3 PAMAM dendrimers, leading to a change of the ζ-potential to 1.1, 9.8, and 12.8 mV. The increment observed in the ζ-potentials was related to the presence of different amine terminal densities from the diverse dendrimers generations [54]. In another study, the developed Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 nanohybrids present a ζ-potential of 36.3 mV, while the GQDs show a value of −20.8 mV, probably from the presence of surface –COOH groups. After grafting GQDs on the surface of triazine dendrimers, the ζ-potential reached the value of −55.4 mV due to the reduction of the number of amino groups as a consequence of amidation between the dendrimer and GQDs [56]. G5 PAMAM-Acetylated-EPI with a ζ-potential of 11.6 mV and anionic CQDs of −18.5 mV interacted electrostatically, changing the ζ-potential to 8.7 mV, which suggested the efficient grafting of the negatively-charged CQDs onto the dendrimer [59]. In another work, negatively-charged CDs were obtained with a ζ-potential of −25.6 mV and combined with G5 PAMAM-TPGS, which presented a ζ-potential of 15.2 mV, and the successful complexation was confirmed due to a change in the ζ-potential (−5.7 mV) [62]. Anionic CDs were synthesized, presenting a ζ-potential of −5.7 mV, and were combined with naked G4-G6 PAMAM dendrimers presenting a positive charge of 2.7, 5.9, and 8.9 mV. After non-covalent interaction, the values obtained for the G4-G6 CDs@PAMAM nanohybrids were −0.41, −0.77, and 1.6 mV, respectively. This increase of ζ-potential when compared to naked CDs suggested the self-assembling with the PAMAM dendrimers [63].

The nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers may have potential applications in biomedicine. In fact, from the literature, it is evident that a significant number of these nanohybrids were developed with that purpose in mind, especially for drug and gene delivery, bioimaging, and antimicrobial uses. The main results and conclusions from those studies are presented and discussed in the following sections.

Drug delivery nanosystems have enormous potential for cancer treatment, providing the patient with precise therapeutic effects and minor damage to the healthy tissues [70–73]. Therefore, a significant amount of work regarding nanohybrids was focused on potential anticancer applications. The CDs gifted the nanohybrid with the luminous capacity to follow its transport at the cellular level, and the dendrimer's void space allowed the encapsulation of anticancer drugs while the terminal groups were functionalized to receive target ligands. The developed nanoplatforms presented multifunctionality, cellular imaging capacity, drug encapsulation, surface functionality, stimuli-responsiveness, and therefore carrying, tracking, and delivering wisely, confirming the potential for cancer theranostics. Matai et al. [59] (Fig. 9 A) encapsulated EPI-epirubicin in G5 PAMAM-Ac (acetylated) dendrimers and, by non-covalent interaction, these were combined with CQDs, forming CQDs@EPI⊂G5-Ac85 nanohybrids. The prepared nanohybrids were tested for EPI release, intracellular imaging, and cell apoptosis in MCF-7 (Michigan Cancer Foundation-7) cancer cells. Drug-release studies made in a 48-h period at different pHs show a higher EPI release for pH 5.5 (90%) than at physiological pH (7.4). The nanohybrids also presented respectable anticancer activity against MCF-7 cells since, at 5 μM EPI concentration, almost 70% of cancer cells were killed. Moreover, the cell cycle and DNA damage were examined by flow cytometry, showing that the nanohybrids increased the sub G0/G1 phase and reduced the S phase. It was then possible to conclude from the results obtained that cell death was produced in an apoptotic mode. Ma et al. [53] (Fig. 9B) developed a zwitterionic nanoplatform for cancer treatment based on CDs, peptide dendrimers, and the polymer pCBMA (poly(carboxybetaine methacrylate). The developed system was used to carry DOX, along the potential bioimaging CDs (CD-D). The pCBMA(CD-D/DOX) system resisted opsonization and entered the tumor cells, releasing DOX through a smart redox-responsive approach. In vivo studies using Bagg Albino (BALB/c) mice with subcutaneous mammary carcinoma, showed high tumor inhibition rates (75.4%) compared to DOX.HCl (42.3%).

As for Feng and co-workers [52], which prepared conjugates between CQDs and G3 PAMAM dendrimers, the drug release was tested at pH 5.5 and 7.0 using the DOX-drug model, resulting in 70 and 48% drug release, respectively, after 70 h of exposure, showing a pH-dependent release and potential anticancer applications. Regarding the work of Li and collaborators [61] (Fig. 10 A), CDs/DOX complexes were prepared by hydrogen bonding and further conjugated with G5 PAMAM-RGD-TPGS forming complexes by electrostatic interaction for enhanced chemotherapy purposes. Also, in this case, drug release tests at pH 5.5 and 7.4 resulted in pH-sensitive behaviour with higher amounts of DOX released at acid pH. These nanohybrids also presented anticancer activity and target specificity in A549 cells (adenocarcinoma human epithelial cells), particularly at 35 μg/mL concentration, resulting in 9% cell viability and inhibiting cancer cell growth. The introduction of RGD in the nanohybrids allowed the specific targeting of CDs/DOX@G5-RGD-TPGS complexes to integrin αvβ3-expressing A549 cells. In the work of Pooresmaiel et al. [55], a nanohybrid based on GQDs and PAMAM dendrimers was developed and used to deliver DOX into MDA-MB 231 cells. The GQDs-PAMAM nanohybrid was previously functionalized with cyclodextrin to increase the biocompatibility of the system, forming GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD. Then, the dendrimer structure was loaded with 62% of DOX, the drug-release was pH sensitive, and the final complex GQDs-PAMAM-β-CD(DOX) presented antitumor efficacies. In another study, Karimi et al. [56] (Fig. 10B) prepared new triazine dendrimer-conjugated GQDs immobilized on Fe3O4@C@NH2 core-shell microspheres (Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs) that were used as a potential carrier for DOX. Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres presented a loading-efficiency of 63.1% and, after exposure to pH 5.0 and 7.4 for 72 h, resulted in a drug release of 65.1 and 28.5%, respectively. Moreover, the cell viability of A549 cells treated with Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX at the concentration range of 2–32 μg/mL decreased, with 3% cell viability obtained after exposure to a concentration of 32 μg/mL. The increased cytotoxicity towards A549 cancer cells was due to DOX efficient transport by the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres.

The same group [57] developed a novel targeted delivery system based on iron oxide nanoparticles, triazine dendrimers, and CDs (Fig. 11 A). First, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were modified with TEOS (tetraethyl orthosilicate), and the surface was then used to grow - G2.5 triazine dendrimers that were further modified with folic acid-derived CDs. The prepared Fe3O4@SiO2–G2.5–CD–DOX complexes presented an encapsulation efficiency of 84.3% and showed pH-dependent release, with 88.1% of DOX being released at pH 5.0 after 72 h. The complex also presented significant cytotoxicity towards A549 cells after 48 h compared to free doxorubicin. In summary, the system developed by this group represented the formation of enhanced drug delivery systems based on CDs and triazine-type dendrimers. Li et al. [62] (Fig. 11B) developed a nanoplatform based on CDs and PAMAM dendrimers named (G5-TPGS@y-CDs)-DOX complexes to treat multi-drug resistant tumors. The surface modification of G5 PAMAM with d-α-tocopheryl polyethylene glycol 1000 succinate (TPGS) limited the mechanism of multi-drug resistant (MDR) cancer cells by inhibiting P-glycoprotein, increasing the anticancer effect in MCF-7 and MCF-7/ADR-adriamycin resistance cells. Furthermore, the sonoporation effect of ultrasound-targeted microbubble destruction technology further improved the anticancer and chemotherapeutic efficacies.

To the best of our knowledge, the use of nanohybrids made of CDs and dendrimers as gene carriers were only studied by our team and other two research groups. The nanohybrids allowed the formation of stable complexes that acted as efficient nucleic acids nanocarriers and prevented enzymatic digestion. Ghosh et al. [54] used CDs-PAMAM G3-RGDS (Arg-Gly-Asp-Ser oligopeptide) conjugates to condense and complex pDNA (encoding GFP – green fluorescent protein) and studied the transfection efficiency in MDA-MB-231 (human Caucasian breast adenocarcinoma) cells (Fig. 12 A). The CD-PAMAM G3-RGDS/pDNA complexes were formed at different weight ratios and presented respectable DNA condensation properties. The CD-PAMAM G3-RGDS/pDNA complexes at the 20-wt ratio, when exposed to MDA-MB-231 cells for 4 h resulted in green emission, indicating GFP expression. The CDs-PAMAM G3 conjugates presented DNA condensation capabilities and even protected the gene from enzymatic digestion. Furthermore, the low toxicity up to 500 μg/mL concentration, blood compatibility, and improved gene transfection efficiency showed the promising use of CD-PAMAM G3-RGDS conjugates as gene delivery systems for TNBC (triple-negative breast cancer) gene therapy. Our group [63] prepared G4-G6 CDs@PAMAM nanohybrids by non-covalent attraction and tested their DNA condensation and delivery capabilities (Fig. 12B). The outcome obtained from the PicoGreen® assay showed nanohybrid's capacity to condense pDNA compared to the pristine PAMAM dendrimers. The studies with HEK 293T (human embryonic kidney) cells using GFP and fluorescence microscopy clearly showed the capability of the nanohybrids to deliver DNA at high transfection efficiency. The luciferase assay also indicated that the nanohybrids were better delivery agents than CDs alone. When compared to other dendrimer generations, the G5 nanohybrids were the best delivery agents. Recently, Wei et al. [74] developed a nanocarrier based on carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) and G3 PAMAM dendrimers. The G3 CNPs-PAMAM conjugates displayed long-wavelength emissive properties and the ability to condense and deliver small interfering RNA (siRNA) to exert its gene-silencing effect on neuronal PC-12 (adrenal phaeochromocytoma) cells.

Bioimaging is perhaps the most common application of CDs due to their PL, photostability, and low cytotoxicity properties [75,76]. The excitation-dependency behaviour of some CDs resulted in multi-color emissions illuminating cells. The alliance with dendrimers provided the nanohybrid with protection to avoid quenching from the surrounding environment, and the capacity to receive target ligands facilitating the cellular uptake. Importantly, the fluorescence of CDs after dendrimer conjugation can be preserved or even enhanced, thus maintaining the potential for imaging purposes [63]. CQDs were examined as fluorescent probes to monitor the intracellular trafficking of CQDs@EPI⊂G5-Ac85 fluorescent nanohybrids in MCF-7 cells [59]. The authors found that, after 12 h incubation, CQDs were mainly internalized in the cell cytoplasm, which suggested the cytoplasmic region as the main cellular target of free CQDs. In addition, CQDs@EPI⊂G5-Ac85 nanohybrids were also traced in the cell cytoplasm surrounding the nucleus and exhibited colocalization of green and red fluorescence due to the release of both CQDs and EPI, respectively. The obtained results demonstrate the capacity of CQDs to be used as fluorescent tracers for the cellular delivery of therapeutic agents [59]. In a different work, the blue fluorescence from CDs was used to monitor the intracellular tracking of (CDs/DOX)@G5 PAMAM–RGD–TPGS complexes in A549 cells [61]. The cells treated with (CDs/DOX)@G5-PAMAM–RGD–TPGS complexes exhibited blue and red fluorescence signals after 6 h incubation and purple fluorescence in the bright-field merged images due to the colocalization of blue and red fluorescence (DOX). The (CDs/DOX)@G5 PAMAM–RGD–TPGS complexes were found in the cytoplasm and surrounding the nucleus indicating the cellular internalization of the entire complex, allowing the tracking of CDs and DOX. In another study from the same group [62], the cellular internalization of (G5-TPGS@y-CDs)-DOX complexes in MDR cancer cells was monitored using the yellow fluorescence from CDs (Fig. 13 A). After 6 h incubation, the MCF-7/ADR cells exhibited yellow-green (CDs) and red fluorescence (DOX), and the merged images showed orange fluorescence due to the colocalization of y-CDs and DOX. The findings from the described studies confirm the capacity of the nanohybrids to act as a fluorescent probe for fluorescence imaging of cancer cells and for tracking the developed complexes. In one of our recently published works [63] (Fig. 13B) exploring the potential use of G4-G6 CDs@PAMAM nanohybrids for bioimaging purposes, cellular internalization studies were made in HEK 293T cells for 24 h using flow cytometry confirmed the existence of nanohybrids inside cells when they were illuminated with green and red light. The results confirmed the nanohybrids potential for cell imaging purposes and that fluorescence intensity can be controlled by playing with the PAMAM dendrimer generation. In another study, CDs-PAMAM G3 conjugates were modified with RGDs to enhance cellular uptake since MDA-MB-231 cancer cells overexpress the RGD receptor αvβ3 integrin (Fig. 13C). By using confocal microscopy, it was found that CDs-PAMAM G3-RGDS conjugates were equally distributed in the cell's cytoplasm. After excitation at 405, 473, and 559 nm, the conjugates illuminated the MDA-MB-231 cancer cells with blue, green, and red fluorescence, respectively. This result also confirmed the multi-color emission from the developed CDs-PAMAM G3 conjugates, showing potential to be used as an imaging agent for TNBC cells [54].

Antimicrobial resistance is a major concern for public health, human lives, and our healthcare systems, by causing a variety of illnesses, and therefore deserving continuous research and development from the academia and industry [77]. The nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers can contribute to this burden, by acting as antimicrobial agents or in combination with antibiotics. In this sense, the group of Ngu-Schwemlein et al. [50] used CDs as a molecular scaffold to enhance the antimicrobial activities of low-generation PAMAM dendrimers. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) showed that the antimicrobial activities of the surface polyaminated CNDs scaffold was effective against both antibiotic-resistant (K. pneumonia 1706, P. aeruginosa 1744, and A. baumannii 1605) and non-resistant strains (K. pneumonia 13883, P. aeruginosa 27853) (Fig. 14 ). Remarkably, they were 4-fold more effective in killing the resistant K. pneumonia strain than the corresponding non-resistant strain. In general, the antimicrobial activity and selectivity of the nanohybrids CNDPAM1 and CNDPAM2, respectively, involving CDs and generation zero and one of PAMAM dendrimers, were similar (the MIC value was between 8 and 32 μg/mL), suggesting that CDs could be helpful in supporting small polycationic amines to enhance their antimicrobial activity by controlling their cationic densities to best interact with the anionic surface of the bacterial membrane. The disruption of the bacterial membrane by the nanohybrids can increase the uptake of antibiotics (tetracycline and colistin) inhibiting the bacterial growth faster. Moreover, the potential use of the intrinsic and enhanced fluorescence from CNDs can endow the nanohybrid system with potential imaging and diagnostic capabilities.

The development of fast and precise detection tools to target specific analytes is a hot scientific topic [78,79]. A significant part of the work on nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers was focused on their potential sensing applications, mainly for bio and chemical sensing. Using the intrinsic fluorescence from CDs (mostly quenched by FRET) combined with the dendrimer's surface functionality allowed the construction of sensitive, stable, and accurate nanohybrids. These nanohybrids regulate and target, important analytes during surgery and postoperative treatment, myocardial infarction, and cancer [67]. A turn-on fluorescence method for the sensitive detection of two polyanions that act as anticoagulant (heparin-Hep) and in cellular differentiation (chondroitin sulfate-CS), based on the electrostatic aggregation of GQDs@PAMAM dendrimer nanohybrids was developed (Fig. 15 A). The anionic surface GQDs were electrostatically attached to cationic PAMAM-Guanidyl, which decreased the fluorescence intensity. When Hep and CS were introduced into the system, they were strongly electrostatically attached to PAMAM-Gu+, separating them from GQDs which recovered their intrinsic fluorescence. Moreover, this turn-on fluorescence method provided an interesting strategy for rapid and sensitive detection of Hep or CS in commercial medicinal solutions [58]. An electrochemical sensor for the detection of cardiac troponin I antibody (cTnI) based on a GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified gold screen-printed electrode was designed (Fig. 15B). The working electrode (Au) was first functionalized with 4-ATP (aminothiophenol) for amine termination, and GQDs and G3 PAMAM dendrimers were immobilized on the modified electrode forming an Au/GQD/PAMAM electrode. An anti-troponin I antibody (anti-cTnI) was placed on the above-modified electrode capturing the antigen (cTnI). The current change was measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The Au/GQDs/PAMAM nanohybrids conjugated with cardiac troponin I antibody biosensor detected at least 20 fg/mL (0.1 fg per 6 μL) of cardiac troponin I in 10 min for risk confirmation of myocardial infarction [51].

Hamd-Ghadareh et al. [66] developed CDs-AuNPs-PAMAM-Antibody nanohybrids and tested their immunosensing abilities to detect the CA125 tumor marker (Fig. 16 A). The CDs were functionalized with ssCA125DNA and the AuNP-PAMAM with CA125 antibody. Upon specific interaction with the analyte CA125 tumor marker, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched and by measuring the FRET signals at different marker concentrations, the minimum detection limit was 0.5 fg/mL. Moreover, the immunosensor also detected ovarian-cancer cells, presenting good sensitivity and specificity, confirming their potential for clinical use. The same group [67] developed another immunosensor to detect CA 15-3 tumor marker (Fig. 16B). First, C-dots were functionalized with CA 15-3 antibody to interact with AuNP-PAMAM-aptamer-CA 15-3 antigen, and the Ab-Ag interaction resulted in the significant reduction of the fluorescence intensity due to FRET between CDs and AuNPs. The proposed immunoassay was further used for CA 15-3 detection in serum samples and CA 15-3 extracts from breast MDA-MB-231 cancer cells, presenting promising results.

The nanohybrids for chemical sensing purposes detected analytes based on fluorescence quenching. Campos et al. [68] developed CQDs@PAMAM dendrimer nanohybrid systems as chemical sensors for detecting chloroplatinate ions in solutions of platinum nanoparticles (Fig. 17 A). The green-fluorescence from CQDs was enhanced after PAMAM dendrimer capping. The response of the nanohybrid was tested in the presence of different metal ions, resulting in 91.5% fluorescence quenching by chloroplatinate ions. The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of the proposed nanohybrid sensor were 657.4 and 2.2 mM, respectively, with a sensitivity of 77.9 nM. Another sensor developed by the same team [60] intended to detect nitroaromatic explosives based on nanohybrids between CQDs and G4 PAMAM dendrimer (Fig. 17B). The target analyte was the 4-chloro-2,6-dinitroaniline, which is known to be a powerful explosives initiator. Based on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs@PAMAM conjugates, it was possible to detect this threatening analyte with calculated LOD and sensitivity limits of 2.6 and 111.6 mM, respectively. The addition of PAMAM dendrimer to the surface of CDs was decisive, increasing the interaction with the electron-deficient groups from the target analyte and leading to a stronger signal.

The CDs and dendrimer conjugates were also used for PL enhancements and solar cells applications. For the PL enhancements, the dendrimer was responsible for increasing the fluorescence of CDs and vice-versa. Zong et al. [49] used AuNPs to enhance the fluorescence of CDs by forming Au-PAMAM-CDs conjugates through an amidation reaction. The PAMAM dendrimer served as a spacer between AuNPs and CDs to enhance the fluorescence of CDs. By optimizing the amounts of AuNPs and CDs linked to PAMAM dendrimer, the PL intensity of CDs was enhanced 62-fold after conjugating with AuNPs and CDs-PAMAM. The developed method could be easily applied to different metal-space fluorophores systems enhancing its respective fluorescence. In a different study, GQDs were used to enhance the PL of PAMAM dendrimers by using PAMAM dendrimers as electron acceptors and GQDs as electron donors (Fig. 18 ). The PL intensity of the PAMAM dendrimers reached its maximum enhancement by a factor of 10.9 with a GQD concentration of 0.9 mg/mL. The generated electrons were injected from the GQDs to the PAMAM dendrimers through the GQD/PAMAM interface due to the smaller work function of the GQDs (3.6 eV), in contrast to the PAMAM dendrimers (4.4 eV). Moreover, since the introduction of GQDs can enhance the PAMAM dendrimers' PL, this approach can provide potential applications for biolabeling and biosensors [64].

Solar cells have regained additional interest in the last years, with the introduction of more efficient and cheap materials and the need to reduce CO2 emissions [80–86]. In 2021, Wu et al. [65] developed, for the first time, luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) based on polymer composites between Si-CDs and Si-dendrimers for potential solar cell applications (Fig. 19 ). To increase the dispersion of the Si-CDs, two different silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2) were prepared. The developed Si-CDs/Si-dendrimer composites were placed on a glass substrate and were cured at room temperature, and the Si-CDs resisted better to the typical aggregation fluorescence quenching. The prepared silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2), presented structural variations of methoxy and phenyl groups, resulting in different performances for the LSCs. The maximum external optical efficiency (ηopt) for D1-based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm) was 7.6%, representing an increase of 4.7 times compared to the control. The molecular structure variations of silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2) resulted in different performances of transparency and ηopt.

To a large extent, preparing and characterizing nanohybrids based on CDs and dendrimers is challenging as developing CDs with controlled size and surface and specific functional groups to pair with well-defined structures like dendrimers is still somewhat tricky. Although the research on CDs has grown exponentially, there are still unanswered questions. For instance, doubt about the reproducibility of CDs persists. Therefore, we recommend working in a systematic approach by varying several parameters like the reaction time, temperature, and concentration of the starting precursor, to try to reach a higher control on the formation process of CDs and better understand the mechanistic aspects. Another crucial aspect is purification. Lack of consideration of this last aspect can mask the intrinsic PL properties of CDs produced. Indeed, some exceptionally high values of PL and QYs reported result from unappropriated or incomplete CDs purification methodology. Moreover, avoiding ambiguities regarding nomenclature and classification of CDs is also fundamental, and we urge for a clear consensus. Regarding dendrimers, research must focus more on building the structures as simple as possible, with the essential needs to serve a purpose, increase the large-scale synthesis, and endow the dendrimer with intrinsic imaging potential.

Possibly, properties of CDs and their nanohybrids with dendrimers (especially chemical, optical, and biological properties with industrial significance) could be enhanced using big data and machine learning approaches together with robotic processes to optimize reaction conditions and speed up the synthesis process. Not only the properties but also the yields could be enhanced, turning the needed scale-up easier. Different variables could then be assessed more quickly and efficiently, like studying the effect of different types of dendrimers (and dendrimer generations) or doping (e.g., using heteroatoms or metals/metal complexes). These advancements could pave the way for developing novel applications beyond those presented in this review, such as battery systems, lab-on-a-chip technologies, and fast detection tools. The rapid progression of this field depends on increased investment in physical and human resources and greater collaboration between academia and industry. By fostering stronger partnerships, we can accelerate the development of these nanohybrid materials and unlock new and exciting applications.

This work revises the state-of-art in the formation, characterization, properties, and applications of nanohybrid materials based on CDs and dendrimers over the past decade. Despite their potential, the field is still in its infancy and is scarcely explored. Different strategies of synthesis are being assayed to combine both species, from non-covalent (merely using electrostatic interactions) to covalent (using amide coupling chemistry or disulfide bonding) approaches, as well as casting and biorecognition processes. As expected, the properties of the nanohybrids, either in the form of nanoparticles or film, are impacted not only by the synthesis process but also by the source and mode of preparation of the original CDs, the generation and family of the used dendrimers (PAMAM, triazine, peptide, and silicone dendrimers). Interestingly, depending on the type of synthetical approach, the PL properties, conferred by the CDs to the nanohybrid materials, can be modulated by playing with the dendrimer generation. More significant efforts are needed regarding characterization techniques to confirm the nanohybrid nature of these new materials and understand the interactions established within them. These efforts will be crucial for the applications which have been studied for these materials and show promise to reach the market and benefit people's lives.
Ivo Martins: Writing - Original draft preparation, Reviewing & Editing, Helena Tomás: Writing, Reviewing & Editing, João Rodrigues: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing, Reviewing & Editing.
